coredata.dc:title: Biodegradable, anti-freezing and self-healable hydrogel mulch film for weed control
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shan-Shan
Wang, Song-Bo
Chen, Yu
Zhu, Qi-Shun
Lan, Ling-Min
Bu, Huaitian
Hu, Tian
Jiang, Gang-Biao
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogel is widely used in agriculture as water retention agent and fertilizer delivery vehicle due to its highly porous and hydrated structure. Hydrogel loses water gradually and becomes fragile, which greatly hinders its potential application as mulching film for weed control as the weeds can penetrate the weakened hydrogel film easily. Here, we report a self-healable and biodegradable hydrogel (COCu-K) mulching film composed of chitosan derivatives, copper-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) and KCl for weed inhibition. The dehydrated COCu-K hydrogel fragments spread on top of soil self-heal in-situ into an integrate hydrogel film by simple watering, preventing the germination and growth of weeds. SEM reveals the well-dispersed multi-hierarchical structure on the surface of COCu-K hydrogel. The effects of dosages of KCl and Cu-CDs on the hydrogel structure and consequent performances of hydrogel mulching film including self-healing, anti-freezing, weeds inhibition have been explored. The pot experiments illustrated a weed seeds inhibition ratio of 90% and strengthened growth of pepper seedlings induced by the best practice hydrogel COCu0.08-K2.5. Meanwhile, the COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel shows an outstanding degradability with a degradation rate of 63.61±3.26% within eight weeks, and soil containing degraded COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel presents no phytotoxicity. This study provides a facile and viable strategy to design and utilize hydrogel mulching for weed control.

head.ce:title: Biodegradable, anti-freezing and self-healable hydrogel mulch film for weed control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogel is widely used in agriculture as water retention agent and fertilizer delivery vehicle due to its highly porous and hydrated structure. Hydrogel loses water gradually and becomes fragile, which greatly hinders its potential application as mulching film for weed control as the weeds can penetrate the weakened hydrogel film easily. Here, we report a self-healable and biodegradable hydrogel (COCu-K) mulching film composed of chitosan derivatives, copper-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) and KCl for weed inhibition. The dehydrated COCu-K hydrogel fragments spread on top of soil self-heal in-situ into an integrate hydrogel film by simple watering, preventing the germination and growth of weeds. SEM reveals the well-dispersed multi-hierarchical structure on the surface of COCu-K hydrogel. The effects of dosages of KCl and Cu-CDs on the hydrogel structure and consequent performances of hydrogel mulching film including self-healing, anti-freezing, weeds inhibition have been explored. The pot experiments illustrated a weed seeds inhibition ratio of 90% and strengthened growth of pepper seedlings induced by the best practice hydrogel COCu0.08-K2.5. Meanwhile, the COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel shows an outstanding degradability with a degradation rate of 63.61±3.26% within eight weeks, and soil containing degraded COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel presents no phytotoxicity. This study provides a facile and viable strategy to design and utilize hydrogel mulching for weed control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Excessive weed growth is a severe problem in crop production, as the weed can compete with the crop plants for nutrients, space, and water, resulting in the reduced yield and quality of the produce [1–3]. Mulching has been widely used as one of the weed control methods through inhibiting weed photosynthesis [4]. The mulch film made of polyethylene (PE) is the most frequently used due to its simplicity of manufacturing, flexibility, durability, and low cost [5,6]. It has been reported that approximately 128,652 km2 of agricultural land is mulched with plastic films worldwide [7]. However, it is a costly, time-consuming, and laborious task to remove and dispose the end-of-life non-degradable PE mulch film from the soil [8]. When improperly handled, the residual PE debris in the soil leads to microplastic accumulation that dramatically interferes with the bulk density, porosity, and metabolism of soil, resulting in the deterioration of soil quality, which severely affects the crop yield [9,10]. Therefore, developing biodegradable mulch has aroused extensive attention worldwide to achieve sustainable agriculture.

Biodegradable mulch has the advantages of degradability and environmental friendliness [11], and has been considered as an ideal substitute for PE. According to the prediction of Haapaka’s group, the demands for biodegradable mulch will reach USD 64.3 million by 2024 [12]. The current mainstream degradable mulch films are categorized into four types, they are degradable plastic, paper-based mulch, organic layer mulch and liquid mulch [13]. Many studies have verified that degradable plastic mulch presents comparable agronomic functions as PE mulches. However, the degradation rate of degradable plastic mulch is easily influenced by the climate, and the degradation normally takes months to years which is inconsistent with the crop growth period [14,15]. Both paper mulch and organic mulches (e.g., straw, wood chips, gravels) derived from environmentally friendly biopolymers are renewable with bio-benign degradation products, but they still suffer from the drawbacks of poor mechanical properties, difficult application in the field, easy erosion by the soil, and ambiguous weed control efficiency [15–17]. Recently, liquid mulch film or spray coating has attracted much attention because of its facile application and versatile fitness to diverse field geometries and crops [18–21]. However, the evaporation of moisture from the generated mulch film easily causes surface cracking, making it vulnerable to weed emergence, thus reducing its weed control efficiency and useful lifespan. Therefore, it is urgent and well-justified to explore new residue-free mulch.

Hydrogel has a three-dimensional cross-linked network with a highly porous and hydrated structure, and has been used widely in agriculture as water holding reservoir, nutrient mobilizer, seed coating agent, and carrier for fertilizers or pesticides to facilitate seed germination and enhance crop yield [22,23]. Hydrogels derived from acrylic acid and its derivatives are the most studied hydrogel materials in agriculture due to their facile fabrication, low cost, and superior water absorption and retention properties [24,25]. However, acrylic based hydrogels are difficult to degrade in the soil and the degradation products have potential biological toxicity, making these hydrogels unsuitable for agricultural applications in the long term. Though chemical modification of acrylic hydrogel can improve its biodegradability and biosafety, it is far from satisfactory [26,27]. From the perspective of bio-safety, biodegradability and environmentally friendliness, pure bio-based hydrogel fabricated from natural polymers (cellulose, chitosan, and alginic acid) is an ideal substitute for fossil-based hydrogel [28]. For instance, a series of eco-friendly multifunctional chitosan-based hydrogel were developed and used as superabsorbents, sustained-release agent, and bacteriostatic agent [29–31]. An alginate-coated gelatin-cellulose nanocrystal hydrogel was tested as fertilizer carrier [32]. Supramolecular hydrogel consisting of alginate and carboxymethyl chitosan presented remarkable effect on enhancing water retention of the soil and reducing leach of hymexazol [33].

Although biobased hydrogels demonstrate promising properties in diverse applications in agriculture [32,34], the utilization of hydrogel as mulch film to inhibit weed growth is still in its infant stage, which is mainly ascribed to the inevitable dehydration in the air and the consequent brittlement and cracking of hydrogel [35]. The cracked hydrogel mulch film has weakened weed suppression effect as the weeds can penetrate and grow in the incomplete-covered field through the cracks, deteriorating the growing environment of crops, which results in poor crop yield [36]. To maintain the integrity of mulch film thereby to realize the full coverage of field, the conventional measures for mulch film are to enhance the mechanical properties and ductility [5,37]. However, for the hydrogel mulching film, the cracking of film is a joint effect of dehydration and insufficient mechanical strength. It is therefore highly desired to construct biobased hydrogels that have good water retention to slow the dehydration and postpone the consequent cracking in the natural environment. Moreover, facile strategy of enabling the restoration of cracked hydrogel back to one integrate film will help prolonging the life span of the hydrogel mulching film to the whole crop production season. Self-healing hydrogel can automatically heal damages and restore itself to the initial state [38], using as mulching films, the cracks can be reconnected to one intact film. However, little study has been conducted on biodegradable natural hydrogel with self-healing property in agricultural applications like mulch film.

The aim of this research is to develop a biodegradable and biosafe hydrogel mulch that inhibits weed germination and growth during the whole crop production season by self-healing of cracked hydrogel film. Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS) is a water-soluble chitosan (CS) derivative showing advantages in fabricating self-healing hydrogel [39,40]. Here, a self-healing hydrogel, named as COCu-K, was consisted of CMCS, copper-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) and potassium chloride (KCl). As depicted in Fig. 1 , fragments of dehydrated COCu-K hydrogel are applied onto the soil first, by water sprinkling the fragments spontaneously heal into an intact hydrogel film in-situ on the soil. The full-coverage of soil surface and slow-released Cu2+ ions vest the COCu-K hydrogel outstanding ability of weed inhibition. The KCl particles give COCu-K hydrogel excellent anti-freezing property with a frozen temperature as low as -18 °C. The multi-hierarchical structure with nano-flower clusters in COCu-K hydrogel effectively enhances its hydrophobicity, contributing to the strengthened soil erosion resistance. The pot experiments were carried out to investigate the ability to inhibit weed seeds germination, weed growth and evaluate the ecological toxicity of COCu-K hydrogel.

Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS) was purchased from Shanghai Dibai Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Chitosan oligosaccharide (COS, Mw 800–1000 Da) and chitosan (Mw 50000–60000 Da) were provided by Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. 4-formyl benzoic acid (4-FA), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), tetraethylene pentaamine (TEPA) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Dicyclohexyl carbon diimide (DCC) was purchased from Shanghai Bide Medical Technology Co., Ltd. Citric acid, acetone, copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2 H2O) and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Pastoral soil (soil moisture content: 10 %) was collected from a farmland in Guangzhou, China. The weed seeds and Chinese flowering cabbage (Brassica campestris L. ssp. chinensis var. utilis Tsen et Lee) were purchased from Jiangxi Dukourenjia Co., Ltd. and Guangzhou Changhe Co. Ltd., respectively. The seedlings of pepper (Capsicum annuum L. var. conoides (Mill.) Irish) were purchased from Guangdong Xinxinran Co., Ltd.

OCS was prepared following the procedure reported in our previous work [41]: COS, 4-FA, DMAP, DCC were charged into 120 mL DMSO at mass concentrations of 20.83 mg/mL, 37.5 mg/mL, 0.8 mg/mL and 14 mg/mL, respectively. The above mixture was then stirred for 24 h to achieve a homogeneous solution, into which deionized water was added, and the mixture was kept still for 12 h before filtration. The filtrate was washed sequentially with acetone and deionized water to remove unreacted chemicals before being freeze-dried to obtain OCS. The synthesis route of OCS is shown in Fig. S1.

Cu-CDs was prepared according to a previously reported method [42], and the synthesis route of Cu-CDs is depicted in Fig. S2. The first step was to synthesize CDs using chitosan as carbon precursor by hydrothermal method. 0.04 g of chitosan was dissolved in 40 mL of 1% acetic acid solution, followed by addition of 0.16 mL of TEPA. The mixture was stirred for 5 min to form a homogeneous solution, which was transferred into a 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. The autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature, and yellow colored carbon dots (CDs) solution was obtained.

Subsequently, 40 mL CDs solution was mixed with 40 mL 0.1 M CuCl2 aqueous solution, into which additional water was charged to make a mixture of 160 mL. 80 g NaOH was then added into the mixture, and the mixture was heated to 70 °C before addition of 1.3 g NaBH4 under constant stirring. The reaction was conducted under stirring for 6 h to form Cu-CDs. The generated black precipitates were separated by centrifuging the reaction mixture at 8000 rpm/min (HC-3518, ZONKIA, China) for 10 min, and then were washed with ultra-pure water and anhydrous ethanol six times. Cu-CDs was obtained.

COCu-K hydrogels were prepared according to our previously work with modest modifications [41]. The compositions of prepared hydrogels, namely contents of CMCS, KCl, OCS, Cu-CDs, are summarized in Table 1 . The preparation procedure is as follows: firstly, CMCS and KCl were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, and the mixture was stirred 30 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Secondly, OCS solution (0.06 g OCS was ultrasonically dispersed in 2 mL deionized water in 15 min) was added dropwise to the CMCS/KCl solution under stirring, and the CMCS/KCl/OCS solution was kept reacting for 2 h under constant stirring. Thirdly, Cu-CDs solutions with diverse concentrations were prepared with ultrasonic dispersion of 0.004 g to 0.012 g Cu-CDs in 1 mL deionized water in 15 min. Finally, Cu-CDs solution was added dropwise into the CMCS/KCl/OCS solution under stirring. The mixture gelled into COCu-K hydrogel after 3 to 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of chemical and morphological structure of COCu-K hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The COCu-K hydrogel samples were freeze-dried before examination. The FTIR measurements were performed on an FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) in a wavelength range of 4000–400 cm−1 at room temperature. The crystal structure of COCu-K hydrogel was analyzed by an X-ray diffraction analyzer (XRD, Ultima IV, Japan) in a 2θ range of 10°- 80° with Cu Kα radiation. The morphology of hydrogels was examined on a scanning electron microscope (SEM, XL-30-ESEM, FEI, America). The freezing temperature of COCu-K was characterized by a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC 8000, PE, Germany). The thermal stability of dried COCu-K hydrogel was examined by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (STA449C, NETZSCH-Geratebau GmbH, Germany). The contact angles of dried COCu-K hydrogels were measured on an OCA20 PRO goniometer (Dataphysics, Germany). The mechanical strength of dried COCu-K hydrogel was examined on a universal testing machine (CMT-1203, Zhuhai Sust Electrical Equipment. Co., Ltd., China). The rheological properties of the restored COCu-K hydrogels were studied using a stress-controlled Anton Paar modular compact rheometer (MCR 302, Anton Paar GmbH, Austria). The cumulative release of copper ions in PBS solutions with varying pH values were monitored by Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometer (Z-2700, HITACHI, Japan). Detailed operational procedures for all characterization have been supplemented in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water absorption and retention ability of COCu-K hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
The hydrogel samples were dried in an oven at 65 °C for 15 h before examination. The absorption analysis was conducted by immersing dried COCu-K hydrogel in PBS solution (pH 6.0, 7.0 and 8.0) at 25 °C until equilibrium swelling. At predetermined time intervals, the samples were fished out and weighed after removing excess water on the hydrogel surface. The experiment was carried out in triplicates, and the following equation was used to define the absorption ratio:

where Wt is the weight of the swollen hydrogel at time t, and W0 is the weight of the dry hydrogel.

The water retention ability of COCu-K hydrogel was determined by the weight change of hydrogel stored in a climate chamber at 40 °C under 40% relative humidity for 24 h. After 24 h storage, the hydrogel samples were weighed after removing excess water on the surface. The experiment was carried out in triplicate, and the following equation was used to define the water retention ratio:
(2)

where Wt is the weight of hydrogel after 24 h storage, and W0 is the initial weight of the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of COCu-K hydrogel mulching film on the germination of weed seeds
ce:section.ce:para:
The inhibition effects of COCu-K hydrogel films with different compositions on the germination of weed were examined. Seed trays with 5×5 holes of 50×50 mm2 were used. Among the 25 holes, 15 holes located in columns 1, 3 and 5 were seeded with approximately 50 weed seeds each and the other 10 holes were kept blank to facilitate the further observation. To the holes with weed seeds, fragments of dried hydrogel film of 30 mm diameter were sprinkled onto the surface of the soil, followed by hydrating with 25 mL water to form continuous hydrogel covering the whole surface. The germination of seeds was monitored daily, with images taken on day 0, 7 and 20. The experiment was carried out in triplicate, and the weed germination ratio of individual COCu-K hydrogel was calculated by the following equation:
(3)
W e e d
G e r m i n a t i o n
R a t i o
%
=
G t
G 0
× 100 %
where Gt is the number of holes in which weed germinated at certain day, and G0 is the total amount of seeded holes, which is 15 in the present case.

The plant safety of COCu-K hydrogel was evaluated by monitoring the growth of pepper seedlings protected by the fresh COCu-K hydrogel film and the germination of Chinese cabbage seeded in the soil containing “last-season” COCu-K fragments. Pepper seedlings (approximate 50 mm tall) were planted individually into flowerpots of 160 mm diameter. To each pot, flakes of dried COCu-K hydrogel film of 90 mm diameter was sprinkled, which was restored into hydrogel by hydration to cover the exposed soil surface. The growth of the pepper seedlings was followed for 40 days by measuring the height of each plant, and the height increase was calculated by using equation (4):
(4)

where Ht is the height of pepper plant after 40 days, and H0 is the initial height of pepper seedling. For each COCu-K hydrogel composition, three parallel experiments were conducted, and its effect on the growth of pepper seedlings was evaluated by the average height increase.The safety of “last season” COCu-K mulch film to the next season crop was examined by the germination of Chinese flower cabbage seeds in the soil containing COCu0.08-K2.5 gel. First, the soil was covered by COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel for 90 days. Second, the fragmented gel was mixed into the soil, into which the Chinese flowering cabbage seeds were sowed. The germination ratio and growth of Chinese flowering cabbage seed were recorded. The experiment was carried out in triplicates, and the following equation was used to define the germination ratio of the seeds:
(5)

where Gt is the germination amount of cabbage seeds, and G0 is the amount of all planted cabbage seeds.

The degradation assessment of COCu-K hydrogel was performed according to the procedure reported in literature [30]. The dried COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel was completely buried in the soil at depth of 10 cm in a pot containing 20 cm deep soil (pH 7.0, soil moisture 30 %), and the pot was placed in a climate chamber of 25 °C and 60 % RH. The hydrogel sample was carefully removed from the soil every 7 days. The collected sample was repeatedly rinsed with water to remove impurities that were adhered to the gel surface, and then dried at 50 °C until a constant weight was achieved. The degradation ratio was calculated by the following equation:
(6)
D e g r a d a t i o n
R a t i o =
W 0
-
W t
W 0
where Wt is the weight of COCu0.08-K2.5 gel at time t, and W0 is the initial weight of the dried COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel film. The experiment was carried out in triplicates, and the average value was calculated.

The COCu-K hydrogels were comprised of CMCS, OCS, Cu-CDs, and KCl. The structures of dried COCu-K gels were characterized by FTIR (Fig. 1d) and XRD (Fig. 1e). The spectrum of pure COCu gel (COCu0.08-K0) shows great consistency with those in our previous study, with several characteristic peaks [41]. The peaks located at 3430 cm−1 and 2890 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –CH2 groups, respectively [43]. The peak at 1620 cm−1 was ascribed to the overlap stretching variations of –C = O group in the OCS and CMCS. The introduction of KCl leads to a shift of featuring peak of C = O stretching in the –COO- from 1620 cm−1 (COCu0.08-K0) to 1602 cm−1 (COCu0.08-K5), which may be attributed to the enhanced hydrogen bonds induced by KCl incorporation and the electrostatic association between the cationic K+ and anionic –COO- of CMCS [44]. In the XRD spectra of all COCu-K samples, no feature peaks of Cu-CDs (43°, 52° and 78°) are presented, indicating the consumption of Cu-CDs in the COCu-K hydrogels [41]. The new diffraction peaks emerge at 28.8°, 40.9°, 50.5°, 59.0°, 66.8°, and 74.1° in the spectra of COCu-K hydrogels, and the peak intensities increase with the enhanced KCl contents. The peaks can be assigned to the crystal panes of (200), (220), (222), (400), (420) and (422) of KCl (JCPDS 00–001-0786), respectively, illustrating that KCl exists in the cubic crystal form in the COCu-K hydrogel.

The gelation times of all hydrogel samples were detected by the tilt method. The snapshots of the reaction mixtures taken at 3, 8 and 10 h are shown in Fig. 2 a. All the gel precursor mixtures are flowable solutions with yellow color. With the elapsing of time, the yellow-colored precursor mixtures solidify into blue-green hydrogels. The color transition from yellow to green and consequently blue is attributed to the slow release of Cu2+ ions that have blue color. With the enhanced concentration of Cu2+ ion, the yellow color of the precursor solution is neutralized into green and shifts to blue eventually. The images show that three COCu-K precursors containing diverse concentrated KCl (K0.5, K2.5 and K5) change color earlier than COCu-K0, suggesting a faster release of Cu2+ ions in the presence of KCl. Meanwhile, the precursors with KCl solidify faster than the counterpart without KCl, with COCu-K5 gels the fastest in less than 3 h. The gelation times of COCu0.08-K0, COCu0.08-K0.5, COCu0.08-K2.5, and COCu0.08-K5 are 600±23, 452±22, 161±12, and 116±13 min, respectively (Fig. 2b). As has been presented in our previous work, the gelation mechanism of COCu hydrogel is the crosslinking of chitosan derivatives by Cu2+ ions, thereby the gelation time is determined by the releasing rate of Cu2+ [41]. The rapid color shift and gelation of COCu-K indicate that KCl promotes the release of Cu2+ ions from Cu-CDs and subsequently accelerates the gelation of COCu-K. To further determine the accelerating mechanism of KCl, we investigated the gelation of four COCu hydrogels containing NaCl, NH4Cl, KNO3, and K2CO3 to validate whether it is K or Cl ions that trigger the release of Cu2+ ions, and the molar concentrations of K or Cl ions were the same as that of KCl in COCu0.08-K2.5. As shown in Fig. 2c, the gelation times of Gel-1 (NaCl), Gel-2 (NH4Cl), Gel-3 (KNO3), and Gel-4 (K2CO3) are 171±3, 145±8, 602±19, and 603±11 min, respectively. Together with the gelation time results of COCu-K hydrogels, it can be concluded that Cl- ions are the driving force promoting the release of Cu2+ thus accelerate the gelation of COCu hydrogel, whereas K+ ions have no significant effect on the gelation process. The gelation of COCu-K hydrogels in the presence of KCl and the corresponding acceleration mechanism by KCl will be discussion in detail in the following section 3.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transition of Cu-CDs to copper compound promoted by KCl
ce:section.ce:para: As noted in the above section, KCl accelerates the dissociation of Cu-CDs in the COCu-K hydrogel precursor and thus the gelation of COCu-K hydrogel. To study the structure change of Cu-CDs in the presence of KCl, the Cu-CDs powder was immersed in KCl solution, and Cu-CDs/H2O dispersion was also made as a comparison. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a, Cu-CDs/H2O suspension depicts no significant color change after 12 d immersion, whereas the color of Cu-CDs/KCl suspension changes from brown to green, suggesting composition change in the suspension. After evaporating the solvent, the solid in the suspension was collected and characterized by XRD and XPS. As shown in Fig. 3b, the collected solid from Cu-CDs/H2O suspension presents three characteristic peaks locating at 2θ of 43.32°, 50.48°, and 74.16° on the XRD curve, corresponding to Cu-CDs (JCPDS 04–0836) [42]. On the contrary, the feature peaks related to Cu-CDs disappear in the XRD spectrum of solid collected from Cu-CDs/KCl suspension, which is accompanied by the emergence of new diffraction peaks. The strong peaks at 28.38°, 40.54°, and 50.22° are assigned to (200), (220) and (222) diffraction planes of KCl (JCPDS 00–001-0786), respectively. In addition, peaks at 16.28° and 58.66° belong to CuCl2, and peaks at 66.4°, and 73.9° are characteristic peaks of CuO and Cu2O, respectively (Fig. 3c). The simultaneous disappearance of Cu and appearance of other compounds preliminarily indicate that Cu-CDs no longer exists in the form of mesocrystal copper after soaked in KCl solution for 12 days, but most likely in the form of Cu(I) and Cu(II).

The solids obtained from Cu-CDs/H2O and Cu-CDs/KCl suspensions were further investigated by XPS to determine the chemical states of the component elements, and the results are demonstrated in Fig. 3d – h.

The total XPS survey spectra of solid collected from Cu-CDs/H2O suspension shows peaks of C, Cu, and O elements, whereas peaks of C, Cu, O, K, and Cl elements coexist on the spectrum of solid from Cu-CDs/KCl suspension (Fig. 3d). The high-resolution spectra of Cu 2p of both solids, Cu-CDs/H2O and Cu-CDs(KCl) are depicts in Fig. 3e. Cu-CDs/H2O has satellite peaks between 939 and 945 eV that are attributed to the copper oxide [45]. Though XRD of Cu-CDs/H2O shows evident characteristic peaks of Cu0 (the metallic copper), no feature peaks of Cu0 (932 eV) can be found in the high-resolution spectrum of Cu 2p of Cu-CDs/H2O. The plausible explanation is that the thin CDs layer coated on the metallic copper blocks the signal of Cu0 [42]. The strong signal peak of C 1s in the full scan curve supports this hypothesis (Fig. 3f).

For the Cu-CDs/KCl, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p (Fig. 3e) is more complicated. Two remarkable peaks can be seen at ∼933 eV and ∼953 eV for Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, confirming the co-existence of Cu(I) and Cu(II) in the solid. Each peak can be further deconvoluted into two sub-peaks. The sub-peaks at 934.3 and 952.6 eV are assigned to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively, implying the presence of Cu+ state in the sample, and the subpeaks at 934.6 eV (Cu 2p3/2) and 954.3 eV (Cu 2p1/2) indicate the existence of Cu2+ state [46,47]. Moreover, the satellite peaks at 939 and 944 eV are significantly enhanced, which are assigned to Cl-Cu-Cl [48]. Unlike the metallic copper and copper oxide form of Cu-CDs in H2O, in KCl solution, most Cu-CDs has transformed to the ionic state (Cu+ and Cu2+).

Fig. 3g shows the high-resolution spectra of O 1s. The peak of O 1s in Cu-CDs/H2O can be deconvoluted into three feature peaks at of 529.9, 531.6, and 533.4 eV, corresponding to the Cu-O bond, –OH and O atom, respectively, which illustrates that copper mainly exists in the form of CuO [45]. For the Cu-CDs/KCl, the feature subpeaks of CuO at 529.9 and 533.4 eV decrease remarkably, further confirm the rare existence of CuO when immersing CDs-Cu in KCl [48]. In Fig. 3h, the Cl 2p peak is fitted into two peaks at 198.3 eV and 199.9 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of Cl 2p3/2 in KCl and CuCl2, respectively [49]. The above results demonstrate that most of Cu element in the Cu-CDs particle are transformed into cupric ion (Cu+ and Cu2+) after being immersed in KCl solution for 12 days. The transformation is induced by the chloride ions, which interact with the Cu element in Cu-CDs and expedite the generation and release of Cu2+ ions from Cu-CDs. The reactions are summarized in Eqs. (7) and (8) [50].

As presented in Section 3.2, COCu-K precursors containing KCl gel are faster than the counterpart COCu-K0 without KCl. Bearing in mind the above reactions of Cu-CDs in KCl, that KCl promotes the generation and release of Cu2+ ions, it is therefore understandable that the presence of KCl in the gel precursor accelerates the gelation process, and the gelation time of COCu-K hydrogels show negative correlation to KCl concentration.

The morphology of dried COCu-K hydrogels was examined by SEM. Fig. S3 and Fig. 4 show the SEM images of dried COCu0.08-K hydrogels with various KCl dosages of 0 to 5 wt%. In the bare hydrogel with no KCl, the COCu-K0 has a 3D network with evenly distributed OCS short rods (Fig. S3) [41]. In the presence of 0.5 wt% KCl, as shown in Fig. 4a, the COCu-K0.5 hydrogel has a smooth interior network with a few plush edges protruding on the surface. The marked area in Fig. 4a is further enlarged and the image is shown in Fig. 4d, in which the plush edges are photoshopped in red to enhance the visibility. Increasing the KCl content to 2.5 wt%, the plush edges grow into flower-like clusters distributing on the surface of COCu-K2.5 hydrogel (Fig. 4b). The enlarged SEM image of COCu-K2.5 in Fig. 4e shows that the flower-like clusters consist of short rods and cubic particles. When the KCl content in COCu-K hydrogel is further increased to 5 wt%, the dried COCu-K5 hydrogel presents a rough surface with spatially distributed cubic particles, no similar brush or flower cluster structure can be observed (Fig. 4c and Fig. 4f). Considering the cubic form of KCl (validated by XRD) and the short-fiber appearance of OCS (presented in our previous study [41]), we therefore assume that the plush and flower clusters are induced by the self-assembly of OCS and KCl, which subsequently interfere with the gelation of COCu-K. In the bare COCu hydrogel, Cu2+ ions coordinate with the –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 groups on both chitosan and OCS, leading to the formation of COCu hydrogel with OCS fibers enhancing the mechanical property. In the COCu-K hydrogel, the free Cl- ions have strong affinity to the coordinate sites of Cu2+ and are therefore attracted to the vicinity of the OCS rods. Meanwhile the K+ ions are associated to the Cl- via electrostatic interaction, resulting in the deposition of cubic KCl on the OCS rods, which present the brush structure as shown in the SEM image Fig. 4d. The elevated KCl concentration in the COCu-K hydrogel induces more deposition of KCl and enhanced self-assembly of OCS and KCl, which forms flower-like cluster structure (Fig. 4e and g). Further concentrated KCl brings in massive Cl- ions, and the excessive KCl cubic particles disperse throughout the hydrogel network, resulting in a rough surface network structure (Fig. 4c and f). The schematic diagram of the gelation mechanism of COCu-K with presumed cluster structure is illustrated in Fig. 4h, during the gelation process of COCu-K hydrogel, the dissociated Cl- ions first attack Cu-CDs and corrode Cu0 into Cu2+ ions, which chelate with functional groups on both CMCS and OCS to form the gel network, the free Cl- and K+ ions associate to the Cu2+ chelation sites successively and subsequently deposit onto the nearby OCS rods to form the cluster structure.

The SEM results indicate that the surface topographies of the hydrogels are closely related to the Cu-CDs contents. We further characterized the morphology of the COCu-K2.5 with different dosages of Cu-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a – f, with increasing Cu-CDs content, the density of flower clusters is enhanced as well, accompanied by sequentially decreased porosity in the gel network.

The flower cluster with massive, tiny OCS spicule covered with KCl cubic crystals composes a multi-hierarchical structure that in analog with that of the lotus leaves, which are conducive to enhance the surface hydrophobicity [51]. The hydrophobicity of hydrogels presenting flower clusters on the surface was evaluated by measuring the contact angle of water, and the effect of density of the flower cluster structure on the hydrophobicity was elucidated. As depicted in Fig. 5g – i, the contact angle of water on the surface of dried COCu-K2.5 hydrogel increases from 24.43±3.92° (COCu0.04-K2.5) to 54.03±1.75° (COCu0.12-K2.5) accompanying the increasing density of flower clusters in the hydrogel, indicating the enhanced hydrophobicity of the hydrogel. Hydrogels with enhanced hydrophobicity have better repellence to water, the COCu-K hydrogel with multi-hierarchical surface structure can therefore better withstand the erosion of rain and prolong the lifetime of mulch in the natural environment [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Frost resistance, mechanical properties, and thermal stability of COCu-K hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: In agricultural practice, the environmental tolerance of mulch is an essential factor in determining its feasibility for long-term application. We systematically investigated the water retention, frost resistance, mechanical properties, thermal stability, and other relevant properties of COCu-K hydrogel film. The water retention rates of diverse COCu-K hydrogels with varying KCl contents were studied by exposing hydrogel at ambient temperature for 24 h to simulate the usage condition of hydrogel mulch, and the results are plotted in Fig. 6 a. The water retention rate of pristine COCu-K0 hydrogel without KCl is 78.79±0.93% within 24 h. With the increased KCl content in the hydrogel, the water retention rate increases to 80.33±0.38%, 85.45±1.07%, and 90.45±0.92%, corresponding to the KCl dosage of 0.5, 2.5, and 5 wt%, respectively. All four hydrogels show excellent water retention ability due to the hydrophilic skeleton derived from carboxymethyl chitosan [52]. Moreover, increased KCl dosage leads to an elevated water retention rate, which can be contributed to the facile binding ability of KCl to water molecules as KCl is a good water-retaining agent [53,54]. The KCl dosage prominently influences the water retention of the COCu hydrogel, the higher the KCl dosage, the better water-retention ability of hydrogel.

When the temperature falls below the freezing point of water (0 °C), most hydrogels inevitably freeze and lose their pristine properties including elasticity and ductility [35,55]. Inspired by the anti-freezing mechanisms found in nature [56–59], integrating the ionic compound KCl into the COCu hydrogel is considered a feasible way to enhance the cold tolerance and improve the anti-freezing ability of hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 6b, the freezing temperature of the COCu-K0 hydrogel is -16.72 °C. With 0.5, 2.5 and 5 wt% KCl presenting in hydrogel, the freezing temperature of the corresponding hydrogel is -17.45, -20.19 and -30.41 °C, respectively, indicating the addition of KCl leads to the lowering of the freezing temperature, which is attributed to the suppressing of ice crystals formation by the salt solution [53,59]. The suppression effect of salt on the formation of ice cores is further validated by the ineffectiveness of Cu-CDs on tailoring freeze temperature. All the COCu-K0 hydrogels containing different amounts of Cu-CDs have freezing temperatures locating around -16.7 °C (Fig. 6c), with no significant decrease with increased Cu-CDs content (0.004 g to 0.012 g). The above results confirm that KCl is the critical factor to hinder the ice core formation in the hydrogel, which in turn lowers the freezing temperature of COCu-K hydrogel and promotes the anti-freezing ability of hydrogel film.

The hydrogels depict different appearance after being kept at -18 °C for 24 h, and the images of COCu0.08-K0 and COCu0.08-K2.5 are shown in Fig. 6d. COCu0.08-K0 hydrogel with no KCl exhibits a form of stiff fiber, whereas COCu0.08-K2.5 is still soft and hangs down flexibly, demonstrating that COCu0.008-K2.5 is not frozen. Considering the conventional outdoor temperature and the fabrication cost as well as the anti-freezing performance of the hydrogel, the optimal dosage of KCl in gels was selected to be 2.5 wt%.

The TGA curves of COCu-K2.5 hydrogel with different Cu-CDs content are shown in Fig. S4a. All three samples experience three stages, namely dehydration, decomposition and carbonization [60]. The decomposition temperature of COCu0.04-K2.5, COCu0.08-K2.5 and COCu0.12-K2.5 hydrogel are all around 255 °C, with remaining weights of 48.05%, 53.24% and 43.99% after carbonization, indicating good thermal stability of COCu-K gels. The increase of Cu-CDs content also results in the enhancement of tensile strength of corresponding dehydrated COCu-K2.5 hydrogels (Fig. S4b), confirming that Cu-CDs plays an essential role in strengthening the stability of the COCu-K structure, which is attributed to the elevated crosslinking site density induced by more Cu2+ generated from Cu-CDs.

The above experiments demonstrate that the as-prepared COCu-K hydrogel fulfills the conventional criteria to be used as mulch film, including desired capacity of water retention and thermostable properties. In the following, the regeneration ability of COCu-K hydrogels will be presented. The rheological characterization of regenerated COCu-K2.5 with different Cu-CDs additions are shown in Fig. S4c – g. As can be seen from the strain sweep curves (Fig. S4c), with increased Cu-CDs content in the hydrogel, the storage modulus (G’) of the hydrogel is enhanced more remarkably than that of the loss modulus (G’’) in the linear viscoelastic region, suggesting an enhanced solid-like behavior of densely cross-linked COCu-K hydrogel induced by high Cu-CDs content [61]. When heating up the hydrogels from 25 to 40 °C in the rheometer, the viscoelastic behaviors of hydrogels show no pronounced change, only slight decrease can be observed (Fig. S4d), which indicates the reliable thermal stability of hydrogel in the conventional application temperature range of mulching film. The self-healing ability of the hydrogels were evaluated by applying alternating strains of 400% (breakage) and 1% (recovery) to the hydrogels under constant oscillation (1 Hz), the breakage-recovery cycle was repeated four times to investigate the robustness of self-healing ability. The breakage-recovery of hydrogels are summarized in Fig. S4e – g. In the initial stage, the hydrogel is subjected to 1% strain, the G′ of all three hydrogels is greater than G’’, exhibiting a gel-like state. After 50 s of low strain load, the hydrogel is impacted by a high strain of 400% for 50 s, in which period the G’’ has a higher value than G’, demonstrating the deconstruction of hydrogel with a sol-like behavior. Subsequently, the strain is set back to a low level of 1%, an instantaneous recovery of hydrogel can be observed from the rheological result with both G’ and G’’ recovering to their initial values. After repeating the damage-healing process four times, the recovery rate of G’ for all three hydrogels is close to 100%, indicating the outstanding self-healing ability of the COCu-K hydrogels. The outstanding self-healing performance vests the hydrogel a capacity to repair itself after being damaged.

The self-healing of COCu0.04-K2.5, COCu0.08-K2.5 and COCu0.12-K2.5 hydrogels with different compositions on the uneven soil surface was tested. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the dehydrated gel fragments are evenly spread on the soil surface, followed by hydrating with suitable amount of water. The mass of gel fragments and water are the same for all three pots. In 60 mins, COCu0.04-K2.5 and COCu0.08-K2.5 spontaneously heal into an intact hydrogel coating on the soil surface, and the recorded self-generation process of COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel can be seen in the Video S1. Whereas the fragments of COCu0.12-K2.5 only swell without connecting and rebuilding into an integrate hydrogel film. According to our previous work, highly crosslinked COCu hydrogel has weakened capacity of dynamic bond reconstruction as the mobility of the gel network is reduced [41], which in the present case results in the incomplete restoration of COCu0.12-K2.5 hydrogel. Besides, the soil particles may also serve as obstacles to blocking the network rebuilding. Both factors contribute to the poor restoration of COCu0.12-K2.5 gel fragments.

The robustness of restoration of COCu-K hydrogels, including the multi-cyclical regeneration of broken hydrogel and the well-maintained mechanical strength, provides COCu-K hydrogel film good tolerance to the exterior stresses brought by the environment. For example, the hails and soil erosion are the main reasons that result in the breakage of mulching film. Unlike the plastic film that needs to be removed and replaced when broken, the COCu-K hydrogel can be easily repaired by rehydration, which has economic and environmental benefits. Moreover, the COCu-K hydrogel mulch film can be applied as dry fragments around the plants, which regenerate in-situ into integrated mulching film, thus giving full protection for the plants from weed growth. Compared to the traditional plastic mulching film, the in-site regenerated hydrogel film saves the labor-intensive work of applying, perforating, and disposing after usage, which in turn contribute to the production economy.

As presented above, the COCu-K hydrogel mulching film has superior characteristic over the traditional plastic film from the perspectives of economy and environment. The crucial properties of mulching film, weed inhibition effect and biosafety of the COCu-K hydrogel film will be investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Weed inhibition effect of COCu-K hydrogel mulching film
ce:section.ce:para: The in-situ regeneration of dehydrated hydrogel fragments into mulch film provides the convenience of storage, transportation and use of hydrogel as mulching film materials; and their excellent self-healing property realizes the no-gap coverage of soil by the hydrogel mulch, thus ensure the functional effectiveness of hydrogel mulching film. The hindering effects of three COCu-K2.5 hydrogel mulch films on the weed germination and growth were evaluated, with the bare soil as control. As can be seen from Fig. 7b, on day 7, the seeds sewed in holes containing bare soil germinate into seedling, whereas in the holes covered by COCu0.04-K2.5 and COCu0.08-K2.5 mulch films, no germination can be observed. On day 26, most green seedlings in the bare soil (control group) withered. A few seedlings can be seen growing in the holes covered with COCu-K hydrogels, with the most seedlings appearing in COCu0.12-K2.5 covered pot. As noted earlier, COCu0.12-K2.5 hydrogel fragments cannot restore into an intact film in situ, leaving several slits uncovered by the hydrogel, from where the seedlings thrive, and no seedlings grow in the hydrogel covered areas. The germination and growth of weeds in the COCu0.08-K2.5 and COCu0.04-K2.5 covered pots are rare, and the spatially grew weeds are probably due to the relatively thin or weak hydrogel film area formed during restoration process induced by the uneven soil surface. The germination rate of weed in the pots covered by COCu0.04-K2.5, COCu0.08-K2.5, and COCu0.12-K2.5 is 13.23±2.31%, 6.73±0.91% and 33.34±1.51%, respectively (Fig. 7c). These results preliminary illustrate that all three COCu-K2.5 hydrogels can inhibit the germination of the weeds, and the weed inhibition capacity is affected by the strength of the hydrogel film and the coverage rate on the soil, which can be tuned by the Cu-CDs dosages of the COCu-K hydrogel as both the film strength and coverage ability are controlled by the hydrogel network density.

The effects of COCu-K protection on the growth of crop and inhibition of weed were studied by pot experiments, using pepper seedlings as subject plants. Six-week-old pepper seedlings with heights of no significant difference were transplanted into polystyrene pots. The soil was collected from the experimental field of South China Agricultural University and used without treatment, therefore weed seeds were expected to be present. The experiment groups were covered with COCu-K2.5 hydrogel fragments with different Cu-CDs dosages, and bare soil was used as the blank control group. The growth of pepper seedling was documented by measuring their heights on preset days, and pictures were taken by a digital camera on the measuring day. As can be seen from the pictures in Fig. 7d, twenty days after transplanting, the heights of the pepper in all experimental groups are higher than those in the control group, and no weed seedling is found in any experiment groups, while weed grows around the pepper in the control group. Forty days after transplanting, the pepper seedling in the control group partly withers with loss of leaves, and some weed flourishes beside the pepper. On the contrary, in the experiment groups with the soil covered by COCu-K hydrogel, the pepper plants grow higher with leaves becoming bigger, no weeds can be observed in the pots. The heights of the pepper plants have an average increase of 23.8±3.21 cm, 28.2±2.9 cm, 31.5±1.02 cm and 29.8±0.93 cm, for control, COCu0.04-K2.5, COCu0.08-K2.5 and COCu0.12-K2.5 group, respectively (Fig. 7e). These results further reveal that covering soil with COCu-K2.5 hydrogel can significantly inhibit the growth of weed and promote pepper growth. The combination of physical coverage and the slow release of Cu2+ ions from the hydrogel both contribute to the satisfactory weed inhibition. Besides, KCl in the hydrogel serves as a potassium fertilizer and benefits the crop growth [62]. Based on the results of weed seeds germination and pot experiments as well as the consideration of production cost and biosafety, COCu0.08-K2.5 gel is chosen as the optimal recipe for further tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of COCu-K hydrogel and long-term effects of degraded COCu-K film on crop growth
ce:section.ce:para: The COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel mulching film was observed to degrade on the soil in the above weed inhibition experiments. The degradation of hydrogel results in the dissociation of network, which may lead to the release of Cu2+ ions that potentially will stress the crop growth [63]. It is thereby necessary to validate whether the degraded COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel has interferences on crop growth. Fig. 8 a and b depict the germination and growth of Chinese flowering cabbages in the bare soil (control group) and the soil containing aged COCu0.08-K2.5 (experiment group). On day 3, the Chinese flowering cabbages in both groups germinate, and the germination rate is higher in the experiment group (40.83±2.44%) than in the control group (33.07±2.21%). On the 5th day, more cabbage seedlings can be seen in the experiment group, with a germination rate of 66.63±3.85% vs 55.47±2.75% in the control group. On the 7th day of sowing, most Chinese flowering cabbages in both groups have germinated with germinations rate over 90%, and the seedlings are growing, suggesting that the soil treated with gel mulching film has no toxicological effects on the new round of crop growing.

Subsequently, the degradation behavior of COCu0.08-K2.5 was recorded by following the weight change of the dried gel buried in soil, and the results are plotted in Fig. 8c. Specifically, the extents of degradation of COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel after 8 weeks is 63.61±3.26%. Further, the water absorption behavior and Cu2+ release performance of COCu0.08-K2.5 under pH 6.0, 7.0, and 8.0 were systematically explored. As can be seen from Fig. S4h, COCu8-K2.5 hydrogel shows fastest water absorption behavior under the alkalic condition, while the water absorption is the slowest under acidic condition. The equilibrium water absorption ratio of COCu8-K2.5 under three pH conditions are 2348.81±191.31%, 2395.31±74.95% and 2587.44±255.52%, respectively, indicating that the hydrogel has excellent water absorption ability in various pH, which endows the restoration of hydrogel under diverse soil pH, thus enables the utilization of COCu-K hydrogel in versatile soil conditions.

The release of Cu2+ under three pH conditions appears the same trend as shown in Fig. 8d. During the first two days, due to the rapid swelling of hydrogel, a burst release of Cu2+ is observed in all three pH conditions. The release slows down gradually and eventually levels off after 10 days. During the whole investigation period, COCu-K hydrogel releases highest amount of Cu2+ in pH 8 solution and lowest Cu2+ under pH 6. The result of cumulative release of Cu2+ is in excellent consistence of the previous reports on CMCS hydrogel [64]. In alkalic conditions, the carboxyl group in CMCS exists in the form of COO–, and the gel network expands by electrostatic exclusion, which is conducive to the release of Cu2+; whereas in the acidic condition the carboxyl group of CMCS has the form of COOH, promoting enhanced hydrogen bonding formation in the hydrogel network, which effectively block the release of Cu2+ [65]. The cumulative amount of Cu2+ released in the burst release stage is 0.18%, 0.24%, and 0.31% under pH 6, 7, and 8 respectively, and at the end of the cumulative release test, 0.25%, 0.33% and 0.41% of Cu2+ are released by COCu0.08-K2.5. All these amounts are lower than the safety threshold value of 0.5% of Cu2+ for plant growth, confirming the safety of COCu-K hydrogel film on the growth of crops [66,67].

In this work, biodegradable, anti-freezing and self-healable COCu-K hydrogels were fabricated using chitosan derivatives as the main components that crosslinked by controlled released Cu2+ ions in the presence of KCl. The incorporated KCl not only accelerated the Cu2+ released from Cu-CDs and sped up the COCu-K gelation process, but also enhanced the water retention and anti-frozen ability of the hydrogel. A multi-hierarchy structure comprised of assembly of OCS rods, Cu2+ ions and KCl cubic particles on the hydrogel surface improved the hydrophobicity of COCu-K hydrogels, which strengthened the water repellence of COCu-K mulching film to prolong its lifetime in humid condition. The COCu-K hydrogels had robust restoration ability, making it easy to be regenerated repeatedly by hydration during utilization without laborious work of removing and reapplying. When applied as mulching film, the COCu-K hydrogel showed promising weed inhibition, suitable biodegradability and satisfactory biosafety. COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel was found to be the best-practice one in terms of the production cost, gel robustness, and weed inhibition efficiency. 63.61% of COCu0.08-K2.5 hydrogel degraded after eight weeks of soil burial, and the soil containing degraded hydrogel presented a positive effect on the sprouting of crop. The results of this study verify that the self-healing COCu-K hydrogel has excellent durability, weed inhibition, and eco-safety. Compared to the conventional plastic mulching film, the COCu-K hydrogel film has supervisor characteristics from perspective of both economy and environment, endowing it a promising alternative mulching material for weed control.
